_note: Evals for the growth-content-essay skill. Each prompt's <URL> is a placeholder: drop in a real source before running. The 'expectations' encode the CLAUDE.md quality bar so an essay can be graded objectively. Running these is heavy: each case generates a full essay (fetch + research + write + PDF), so run deliberately, ideally one case at a time, with real source links.

evals.prompt: Here's a YouTube growth talk, turn it into an essay: <YOUTUBE_URL>
evals.expected_output: A comprehensive practitioner essay saved as a PDF in essays/ (named 'YYYY-MM-DD - Title.pdf') plus its markdown in essays/essay md files/, built from the full transcript and following the 6-section template.
expectations: The transcript was pulled with the ytsearch tool and saved to transcripts/ as speaker-name-topic.txt.
A PDF is saved to essays/ named 'YYYY-MM-DD - Title.pdf', and the source markdown is saved to essays/essay md files/.

The essay follows the template in order: Source Info metadata, Executive Summary (4-6 sentences), Key Insights & Frameworks (### 3.x subsections), Tactical Playbook, Contrarian / Non-Obvious Takes (3-5 items), Sources & Further Reading.
Heading levels are correct: a single # title, ## for sections, ### for subsections, and section headers are not numbered.
A topic inventory covering the entire transcript was built first, and every distinct topic the speaker covers appears in the essay (no major segment dropped or flattened).
Specific examples, anecdotes, numbers, company names, and the speaker's caveats are preserved, not compressed into single sentences.
There are no em dashes anywhere in the essay.
No body bullet uses the '- **Label:** value' meta pattern; body bullets use '- **Label**: value' or '- **Label.** value'.
Anti-repetition holds: each statistic, quote, and anecdote appears exactly once, with later sections cross-referencing by subsection number instead of restating.
Every Tactical Playbook item cites the Section 3 subsection it derives from and does not re-explain the underlying insight.
Sources & Further Reading lists every external source used, each with a URL and a one-line note on what it adds.
The PDF uses the Inter / InterDisplay fonts and the navy/blue palette with generous spacing (not Helvetica or Arial).

Major claims, quotes, and statistics are traceable to the source: significant ones carry a timestamp (inline in Section 3), and any claim drawn from research is cited in Section 6. No untraceable facts are introduced.

evals.prompt: Can you make an essay from this blog post? <BLOG_URL>
evals.expected_output: A full essay PDF (and markdown) built from the blog's actual content, or, if the blog is paywalled, a request for the pasted full text rather than a guessed essay.
expectations: If the full blog text is not accessible (paywall), the system asks the user to paste the full text instead of fabricating or guessing content.
When content is accessible, a PDF is saved to essays/ named 'YYYY-MM-DD - Title.pdf' with markdown in essays/essay md files/.
The essay follows the 6-section template with correct # / ## / ### heading levels.
Every distinct idea in the blog is covered; nothing substantive is skipped, and the speaker's nuance and caveats are preserved.
Proper nouns, product names, and terminology from the source are not silently changed; anything suspected wrong is flagged to the user, not auto-corrected.
There are no em dashes anywhere in the essay.
Anti-repetition holds: each stat, quote, and anecdote lives in exactly one section.
Sources & Further Reading lists every external source used during research, each with a URL and a one-line annotation.
Prose is plain, clear synthesis (mechanism-first), not a copy of the blog text, and jargon is defined on first use.

evals.prompt: Make an essay from this Maven Lightning Lesson: <MAVEN_URL>
evals.expected_output: A full essay PDF (and markdown) built from a Whisper transcription of the Maven lesson audio.
expectations: The Maven audio was transcribed with the Groq Whisper pipeline (tools/transcribe_groq.py from the Mux signed stream), not by scraping the page or extracting a CC track.
The transcript is saved to transcripts/ and read end-to-end before writing.
A PDF is saved to essays/ named 'YYYY-MM-DD - Title.pdf' with markdown in essays/essay md files/.
The essay follows the 6-section template with correct # / ## / ### heading levels.
Every topic in the lesson is covered proportionally; introductory/analogy material is included, not treated as filler.
There are no em dashes, and no body bullet uses the '- **Label:** value' meta pattern.
Anti-repetition holds and the Tactical Playbook cites its Section 3 sources without re-explaining them.
Sources & Further Reading lists every external source used, each with a URL and a one-line note.

evals.prompt: Combine these two talks from the same speaker into one essay: <YOUTUBE_URL_1> and <YOUTUBE_URL_2>
evals.expected_output: A single combined essay PDF (and markdown) that weaves both sources by theme, deduplicates overlap, and credits both sources.
expectations: Both transcripts are pulled (ytsearch) and read in full before writing.
The two sources are woven together by theme into one essay, not stacked source-by-source.
Overlapping points appear once (deduplicated), keeping the richer version, with later mentions cross-referenced rather than repeated.
Source Info lists both sources, and each source's key timestamps are attached to its claims inline in Section 3.
A single PDF is saved to essays/ named 'YYYY-MM-DD - Title.pdf' with markdown in essays/essay md files/.
The essay follows the 6-section template with correct heading levels and no em dashes.
Material unique to each source is retained; nothing substantive from either talk is dropped.
Sources & Further Reading lists both primary sources plus any external research, each with a URL and a one-line note.
Each claim is attributable to the correct source (no claim is orphaned across the two talks), and major points carry a source locator such as a timestamp.
